confirmed_major.quote: the programs $P_\sigma\cup B_{\mathrm{AAF}}$ and the ASPARTIX encoding $S_\sigma$ have identical answer sets
confirmed_major.issue: Theorem 1 as stated is FALSE, not merely stronger than what was proved. B_AAF (Figure 1 = background_knowledge.lp) contains the choice rules 0{in(X)}1 / 0{out(X)}1, the coherence constraint, and supported/1. The choice rules add spurious answer sets: I ran clingo on the literal statement. For a single unattacked argument a, P_STB ∪ B_AAF has answer sets {in(a)} AND {out(a)} while ASPARTIX stable has only {in(a)}; for the chain b→a, P_ADM ∪ B_AAF admits {in(a),out(b)} — a non-admissible labelling (a's attacker b is unrefuted). What was actually verified (check_equivalence.py, anthem/stb|adm|cmp_learned.lp, both READMEs) is P_σ plus ONLY the two definitions defeated(X):-att(Y,X),in(Y) and not_defended(X):-att(Y,X),not defeated(Y) — no choice rules, no constraint, no supported/1, no defeat/2 indirection.
confirmed_major.fix: Restate Theorem 1 over P_σ ∪ D_AAF where D_AAF is just the defeated/not_defended definitions (the deductive fragment of B_AAF, explicitly excluding the choice rules, the coherence constraint, and supported/1 — which are the learning-time guessing scaffold, not part of the verified encodings). Fix the Figure 3 caption ('on top of B_AAF') the same way, and add one sentence explaining that the choice rules exist only to make labellings guessable during learning and are dropped from the learned encoding.
confirmed_major.amended_fix: The proposed fix is right but incomplete in two ways. (a) Scope: the same "∪ B_AAF" defect also appears in Theorem 2 (Preferred certificate, line 513: P_admissible ∪ B_AAF ∪ {#heuristic...}) — check_heuristic_encodings.py likewise verified the program WITHOUT choice rules (DEFS only), so Theorem 2 must be restated over P_admissible ∪ D_AAF as well, not just Theorem 1 and the Figure 3 caption. (b) Precision: define D_AAF as exactly the two verified rules defeated(X) :- att(Y,X), in(Y) and not_defended(X) :- att(Y,X), not defeated(Y) (using att directly — the verified encodings have no defeat/2 indirection), and state the equivalence as equality of answer sets restricted to the vocabulary {in, out, defeated, not_defended} (or of in/out projections, matching the anthem external-equivalence formulation) rather than unqualified "identical answer sets", since ASPARTIX's concrete encoding may carry different auxiliary atoms. Keep the suggested one-sentence explanation that the choice rules, constraint, and supported/1 are learning-time scaffold (making labellings guessable for ILASP) dropped from the learned encoding.
confirmed_major.verify_note: Verified adversarially and could not refute. (1) The draft (aij_draft.tex lines 415-423) literally states Theorem 1 over P_sigma ∪ B_AAF, and Figure 1 (lines 292-300) defines B_AAF with the choice rules 0{in(X)}1 / 0{out(X)}1, the coherence constraint, supported/1, and defeat/2. (2) I re-ran both counterexamples in clingo: for arg(a) with no attacks, P_STB ∪ B_AAF yields TWO answer sets projecting to {in(a)} and {out(a)} (empty set is not stable, so even the in-projection clause fails); for att(b,a), P_ADM ∪ B_AAF yields the answer set containing in(a), out(b), not_defended(a) — in(a) is guessed by the choice rule, and {a} is not admissible. (3) The verification artifacts confirm the mismatch: analysis/zlatina_theorems/check_equivalence.py line 16 and anthem/{stb,adm,cmp}_learned.lp use ONLY the two definitions defeated(X):-att(Y,X),in(Y) and not_defended(X):-att(Y,X),not defeated(Y) — no choice rules, no constraint, no supported/1, no defeat/2 indirection. So the theorem as stated is false while the verified claim (over the deductive fragment) is true. The proposed fix is correct in direction.

confirmed_major.quote: $\pred{support}\subseteq\mathit{supp}$ and $\mathit{supp}\circ\pred{support}\subseteq\mathit{supp}$
confirmed_major.issue: The mechanized premise (anthem/baf2.ug, line 11) is ∀XYZ (supp(X,Y) ∧ support(Y,Z) → supp(X,Z)) — closure under appending one support step on the RIGHT. Under the function-composition convention for relations ((R∘S)(x,z) ⟺ ∃y S(x,y) ∧ R(y,z)), the draft's 'supp ∘ support ⊆ supp' denotes the LEFT-extension closure ∀XYZ (support(X,Y) ∧ supp(Y,Z) → supp(X,Z)) — a different class of relations, quantified over in the theorem, that the Vampire proof does not cover. The notation only matches under the diagrammatic/relation-algebra convention, which the paper never fixes. Additionally, 'let defeat be defined from supp by the rules of Figure 2' is imprecise: Figure 2 contains the transitive-closure rule (which is dropped in the reduction) and its defeat rules mention support, not supp; and the verified learned side (baf2_*_learned.lp) also includes the defeated/not_defended definitions, which 'the learned program P_σ' alone lacks.
confirmed_major.fix: Replace the composition notation with the explicit first-order premise, e.g. '∀X,Y,Z (supp(X,Y) ∧ support(Y,Z) → supp(X,Z))', matching baf2.ug; and state the two program sides precisely: P_σ plus the defeated/not_defended definitions plus the three defeat rules of Figure 2 with support replaced by supp (closure rule removed).
confirmed_major.amended_fix: The proposed fix is correct as written. Minor refinement: alternatively the composition ambiguity could be resolved with a one-line convention footnote ('we write R∘S for {(x,z) : ∃y R(x,y)∧S(y,z)}'), but stating the explicit first-order premise ∀X,Y,Z (supp(X,Y) ∧ support(Y,Z) → supp(X,Z)) — together with support ⊆ supp — is cleaner and matches baf2.ug verbatim. Also state the program sides precisely: the learned side is P_σ ∪ {the three defeat rules of Figure 2 with support replaced by supp; the closure rule removed} ∪ {defeated(X) :- in(Y), defeat(Y,X); not_defended(X) :- defeat(Y,X), not defeated(Y)}, and analogously the reference side is the guess-style ASPARTIX encoding with the same supp-defeat and defeated/not_defended rules, exactly as in baf2_*_learned.lp / baf2_*_aspartix.lp.
confirmed_major.verify_note: Verified against the artifacts. (1) anthem/baf2.ug line 11 is 'forall X Y Z (supp(X,Y) and support(Y,Z) -> supp(X,Z))' (right-append closure), while the draft's premise at aij_draft.tex:473-474 is 'supp ∘ support ⊆ supp' with \circ used nowhere else and no convention fixed. Under the standard function-composition convention ((R∘S)(x,z) ⟺ ∃y S(x,y)∧R(y,z)), the draft's formula denotes the left-extension closure — a strictly different class of relations (counterexample: support={(a,b)}, supp={(a,b),(b,c)} is right-closed but not left-closed), and the theorem quantifies over all such supp, so under that reading the stated theorem is not the mechanized one. It matches only under the relation-algebra/diagrammatic convention, which the paper never declares. (2) Figure fig:bgbaf (lines 325-328) contains the transitive-closure rule and defeat rules over 'support', not 'supp'; the verified programs drop the closure rule and substitute supp, but the theorem statement never states this substitution. (3) The verified learned side (baf2_{stb,adm,cmp}_learned.lp) is P_σ plus the three supp-defeat rules plus the defeated/not_defended definitions — not 'the learned program P_σ' (which per fig:learned is only the 2-3 in/out rules), and not P_σ∪B_AAF either (no choice rules/constraint/supported). The proposed fix reproduces the artifacts exactly and is warranted for a theorems-focused AIJ paper.

confirmed_major.quote: (\textsc{global}${}>{}$\textsc{within}, $32/53$ cells, $p=0.03$)
confirmed_major.issue: The '32/53' notation is only arithmetically consistent as 32 within-only vs. 53 global-only DISCORDANT cells (exact two-sided McNemar on 32 vs 53 gives p=0.0295 ~ 0.03, verified by computation). Read the natural way -- 'global beats within in 32 of 53 cells' -- the exact binomial p is 0.169 (one-sided 0.084), not 0.03. Worse, 32+53=85 exceeds the 63 total '#cells' shown in Table 6 (10+6+11+5+5+7+19), so the pooled test must run at a finer unit (per-argument cells) than the table's cell column, which the draft never says. A reviewer who tries to reproduce p=0.03 from '32/53' will conclude the statistic is wrong. Act 1 already uses the unambiguous format ('27 learner-only vs. 54 CF2-only cells'), so the draft is internally inconsistent in notation. Same issue is latent in exp2.tex's footer, but the draft inherits it into the main text.
confirmed_major.fix: Rewrite as in Act 1: '(\textsc{global}-only $53$ vs.\ \textsc{within}-only $32$ discordant (graph, labelling, argument) cells, $p=0.03$)', and state that the pooled test unit is the deduplicated argument-level cell, not the (graph, labelling) cells counted in Table 6.
confirmed_major.amended_fix: In aij_draft.tex line 970, rewrite as: "(\textsc{global}${}>{}$\textsc{within}: $53$ \textsc{global}-only vs.\ $32$ \textsc{within}-only discordant units, exact McNemar $p=0.03$)". Additionally state the test unit, e.g. in the Act 3 text or a table note: "the pooled test is run at the deduplicated per-argument level --- one unit per (cell, argument) pair --- which is finer than the (graph, labelling) cells counted in Table~\ref{tab:act3}; hence discordant counts can exceed the table's cell totals." Apply the same rewording to the footer of Real_World_Examples/fastlas_exp/paper/exp2.tex (line 237), including the adjacent "within$<$CF2 $16/33$" which has the identical ambiguity.

Every checkable element of the finding reproduces against the draft and the generating code.

(1) Draft text confirmed: /Users/fdasaro/Desktop/Zlatina/FabioExperimentsMacM4_claude/docs/aij_paper/aij_draft.tex line 970 reads "(\textsc{global}${}>{}$\textsc{within}, $32/53$ cells, $p=0.03$)".

confirmed_major.quote: incompleteness is more damaging than moderate noise at behavioural scale
confirmed_major.issue: B3 is contradicted both by the paper's own E1.2 ('Label noise, not sample size or partiality, is what degrades recovery') and by the v2 data: at behavioural scale (f<=40, successful runs), dropping completeness p=1.0 -> 0.5 at q=0 moves mean MCC_FULL 0.962 -> 0.977 (null/slightly positive), while adding noise q=0 -> 0.1 at p=1.0 drops it 0.962 -> 0.916 and q=0.2 drops it to 0.844. Moreover B3 -- the license the whole Exp2 attribution rests on -- has no quantitative content anywhere: no table, no stated (p, q, f) coordinates for the Guillaume data, and Sec 8.2's conclusion 'the learner is at its recovery limit' is asserted with no number for that limit. This sentence appears to be a stale claim from a pre-rescore analysis in which the test surface was itself partial.

Add a small table to Sec 6: the operating-point coordinates estimated for the human data (cite the estimation procedure, e.g. estimate_operating_point.py), the recovery limit read off the Exp1 surface at those coordinates with a CI, the observed Exp2 accuracy, and the residual attributed to model mismatch. Without this table, C6 is a claim without a contribution.

(1) Replace rather than delete the line-816 sentence: the data support an interaction claim that still motivates the "undecided is the harder signal" point — incompleteness alone is free at q=0 (0.976 vs 0.960 mean MCC_FULL, f<=40) but multiplies the cost of noise (at q=0.1, recovery drops 0.914 -> 0.839 going p=1.0 -> 0.5; at q=0.2, 0.841 -> 0.616). State it as "incompleteness is harmless on clean labels but amplifies noise damage," consistent with E1.2. (2) The operating-point table for Sec 6 cannot yet be written from existing outputs: estimate_operating_point.py (repo root) and docs/EXP2_OPERATING_POINT.md define the estimators (p-hat = commitments/N pooled over participants; q-hat = (1-sqrt(1-2D))/2 from pairwise inter-annotator disagreement D, bootstrap over participants for a CI), but the script has apparently never been run on the Guillaume records — no (p-hat, q-hat) numbers exist in the repo. Run it, then add the table: (p-hat, q-hat, effective f), Exp1 surface lookup at those coordinates with CI as the recovery limit, observed Exp2 committed-only accuracy, residual = model mismatch. (3) Include the caveat the methods doc itself flags: Exp1's surface covers N in [4,8] while the human graphs have N in {3,4,5}, so the N=3 condition is an extrapolation; and update the Sec 8.2 sentence (line 1080) to cite the table's number instead of asserting "at its recovery limit" unquantified.
confirmed_major.verify_note: Verified independently against artifacts/final_synthetic_v2 (3510 rows, GRD excluded, ILASP_TRAIN_SUCCEEDED=1, f<=40): mean MCC_FULL at q=0 is 0.960 (p=1.0) vs 0.976 (p=0.5) — dropping completeness is null/slightly positive — while adding noise at p=1.0 gives 0.914 (q=0.1) and 0.841 (q=0.2). So line 816's "incompleteness is more damaging than moderate noise at behavioural scale" is false on the paper's own corrected metric and directly contradicts E1.2 (lines 670-679). The stale-claim diagnosis is also confirmed: under the pre-rescore partial-test-surface MCC column, incompleteness WAS more damaging (0.960->0.886 for p, vs 0.960->0.914 for q=0.1), i.e., the sentence survives from the analysis the draft's own Sec 5 methodology explicitly repudiates. confirmed_major.quote: incompleteness is more damaging than moderate noise at behavioural scale
confirmed_major.issue: B3's claim is contradicted by the v2 data. At behavioural scale (f<=40): dropping p from 1.0 to 0.5 at q=0 changes mean MCC_FULL from 0.962 to 0.977 (incompleteness alone costs NOTHING, slightly negative cost); adding q=0.1 at p=1.0 costs -0.046 (0.962->0.916). What the data actually shows is a strong p x q INTERACTION: at q=0.2, p=0.5 gives 0.666 vs 0.873 at p=1.0 (a 0.21 gap), i.e. partial labels amplify noise. No table in the draft supports either B3's claim or the interaction: Table 1 pools over p, and the per-p surfaces are deferred to an appendix that is currently a TODO ('the per-p surfaces tell the same story' is also false -- they tell a different, interaction story). E1.2's 'Label noise, not sample size or partiality, is what degrades recovery' is defensible only as a main-effects statement and hides the same interaction.
confirmed_major.fix: Add a p x q (x f) table or figure to the main text (I verified the marginal table: q=0.2 row p=0.5/0.75/1.0 = 0.666/0.842/0.873; q=0 row = 0.984/0.980/0.972). Rewrite B3 as: incompleteness is harmless under clean labels but multiplies the noise penalty (interaction), and re-derive whatever Exp2 reading depended on 'incompleteness more damaging'. Reword E1.2 to name the interaction explicitly.
confirmed_major.amended_fix: The proposed fix is correct; two additions. The data refute it as stated: at f<=40, q=0, mean MCC_FULL is 0.960 at p=1.0 vs 0.976 at p=0.5 - incompleteness alone costs nothing (slightly negative cost) - while adding q=0.1 at p=1.0 costs 0.046 (0.960 -> 0.914). (2) The interaction is real and I reproduced the reviewer's fix-table numbers EXACTLY under Table 1's own convention (failed runs excluded, pooled over f): q=0.2 row p=0.5/0.75/1.0 = 0.666/0.842/0.873 (n=259/359/360); q=0 row = 0.984/0.980/0.972. confirmed_major.quote: shifts recovery by at most $0.026$ with no consistent sign; the residual sensitivity decays with total sample size (mean $|\Delta|$ from $0.047$ at total $20$ to $0.005$ at total $160$)

(1) No test or CI accompanies a null claim; the 0.026 figure is the spread of arm MEANS over ~53 cells, which mechanically shrinks with aggregation. (3) The 'mean |Delta| 0.047 -> 0.005' statistic is not reproducible under any natural definition: arm-mean spread by total goes 0.035 -> 0.005; mean per-cell spread goes 0.133 -> 0.023. confirmed_major.verify_note:
Every element of the finding checks out against the draft and the repo artifacts.

(1) Metric/unit/learner discontinuity — VERIFIED. Exp1's surface (aij_draft.tex lines 595-599, 630-658) is exact-model matching MCC on a balanced 100+100 complete-information synthetic test set, learned by ILASP with the generative choice-rule mode bias (line 1112-1113 confirms ILASP is required for Exp1's hypothesis space). Exp2's primary numbers (Tables tab:act1, tab:act3, tab:aux) are argument-level committed-only accuracy on 495 human responses, with the sigma_H/aux analyses built on FastLAS OPL under the verifier reformulation. No conversion between the two scales appears anywhere in the draft.

(2) Synthetic gate invoked but never reported — VERIFIED. grep finds "gate" only at lines 793 ("we operationalize this as a hard synthetic gate") and 875 ("each pipeline passes the B1 synthetic gate"). No gate table, no metric, no per-variant outcome anywhere, including the appendix (app:campaign, lines 1225-1231, contains only Exp1 campaign material).

(3) B3 recovery limit never quantified — VERIFIED. "Recovery limit" appears at lines 162, 812, 1080. Line 809-819 defines it conceptually ("reading Experiment 1's surface at coordinates matching the behavioural data") but no matched (p,q,f) coordinates are ever stated, and line 1079-1081 asserts "the learner is at its recovery limit" in a section whose numbers are committed-only accuracy (0.648 vs CF2's 0.797) — the MCC-to-accuracy cash-out the reviewer describes, with no number ever read off the surface.

(4) The finding is actually STRONGER than stated. minors.issue: Both chance figures are idealized uniform-guesser values, not empirical baselines. From the processed human-data file in the repo (Real_World_Examples/Final_Processed_Argumentation_Framework_Data.csv): committed responses split 139 in / 124 out, so the majority-class ('always-in') committed-only baseline is 0.529 -- close to 50% here, but that is a lucky fact of this dataset that the draft never establishes; per-condition it ranges 0.44-0.67 (condition F: 0.67, above several learners). For the three-valued metric the majority class (undecided) scores ~0.363, not 0.333. Separately, ILASP's first-phase committed-only 0.206 is far BELOW chance and passes without comment.
minors.fix: Add an empirical majority-class baseline row to Table 4 (always-in for committed-only; majority label for three-valued), cite the observed in/out/undec base rates, and add one sentence explaining the below-chance ILASP first-phase score (systematic undecided predictions counting as errors).

minors.quote: deduplicated to one unit per (graph, labelling, argument) cell
minors.issue: The dedup choice is defensible (it kills the pseudo-replication from participants sharing identical labellings) and the Act 1 p-values reproduce exactly from the stated counts (verified: 27/81 -> 0.004, 14/51 -> 0.002). But it silently changes the estimand: unique response PATTERNS are weighted equally regardless of how many of the 130 participants produced them, and arguments within one labelling are still dependent (multiple arguments per (graph,labelling) enter as separate units). Also 'pre-registered comparator CF2' -- if no registration document exists, this word is an overclaim a referee may ask to see.
minors.fix: State the estimand explicitly ('distinct response patterns, not participant-weighted behaviour'), add a sensitivity analysis at the participant level (McNemar on all 495 responses with participant-level clustering, or a permutation test permuting participants), and change 'pre-registered' to 'pre-specified' unless a registration can be cited.

minors.quote: Sparse results (all cells complete)
minors.issue: False as stated. In artifacts/final_synthetic_v3_sparse/results the clean cells (q=0) have all 10 runs each, but the q=0.1 cells of the declared anchor slice have 1-2 of 10 runs (ADM 1, PRF 1, STB 2, CMP 2) and the PRF p=1.0/q=0.1 cell is entirely absent (116 rows present of the 160 the stated slice implies). The quoted f=60 numbers are from complete clean cells and check out against the CSVs (STB 0.999, ADM 1.000, CMP 0.998, PRF 0.975), but 'recovery matches or exceeds the dense-pool values at matched coordinates' implicitly covers the noisy coordinates that have not finished.
minors.fix: Scope the sentence to what is done: '(clean-label cells complete; noisy cells executing)' and restrict the matched-coordinates claim to q=0, or wait for the slice to finish. Also note that the BAF breadth table necessarily reports the standard MCC column (MCC_FULL is empty in those CSVs) -- harmless at p=1.0 but worth a footnote for artifact auditors.

minors.quote: \usepackage[colorlinks=true,linkcolor=blue,citecolor=blue,urlcolor=blue]{hyperref}
minors.issue: Citation-style note: elsarticle loads natbib in NUMERIC mode by default, so despite the authoryear-formatted \bibitem[Author(year)] labels, all citations render numerically — \citet{guillaume2022} appears as 'Guillaume et al. [18]' in the PDF (verified via pdftotext). This compiles cleanly (natbib uses the labels only to extract author names for \citet), and numeric is AIJ's published style, so it is not broken — but if the authors intended authoryear (the hand-written labels strongly suggest so), it is silently not happening.
minors.fix: If authoryear is intended, add \biboptions{authoryear} after \documentclass (the existing labels are already in the exact format natbib authoryear needs); if numeric is intended, the elaborate [Author(year)] labels can stay (harmless) but plain \bibitem{key} would suffice.
